subject.title: Psychology
subject.description:
IB Psychology is a Group 3 subject in the International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP). The course examines human behavior and mental processes through scientific inquiry, exploring biological, cognitive, and sociocultural approaches to understanding behavior. Students develop critical thinking skills while investigating contemporary psychological research, research methods, and ethical considerations in psychological practice.

The course is available at Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours) and Higher Level (HL, 240 hours), with first assessment in 2027.

Assessment consists of Paper 1 (integration of concepts, content, and contexts through short-answer and extended-response questions: SL 2 hours, HL 2 hours, SL 50%, HL 40%), Paper 2 (application of psychological knowledge to research contexts: SL 1 hour, HL 2 hours, SL 25%, HL 20%), Paper 3 (HL only: research methods and data analysis, 1 hour, 20%), and Internal Assessment (research proposal development: SL 25%, HL 20%).

strengths: Clear statement of the investigationâs aim and strong justification for replicating schema theory research in a classroom setting
Explicit explanation linking schema theory to practical and theoretical importance beyond the original office study
weaknesses: Hypothesis variables (IV and DV) are identified but lack precise operational definitions
Minor inaccuracies and vagueness in the historical description of schema theory limit theoretical clarity
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The introduction demonstrates a clear aim and relevance (band 5/6) and a solid theory-to-investigation link (band 5/6), but the hypothesis section lacks operational detail (band 3/4). Balancing these, the criterion merits a midârange score of 3/6.

strengths: Appropriate choice and explanation of an independentâmeasures (betweenâsubjects) design to prevent carryâover effects
Justified sampling technique (voluntary response) with rationale for improved generalizability
weaknesses: Procedural description is incomplete (e.g., cutâoff text, missing environmental controls)
Key participant details (age, gender, sample size) are omitted, hindering evaluation of representativeness
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The exploration section explains design choice and sampling reasoning, meeting the 3â4 band, but lacks full procedural detail and participant demographics. This performance aligns with a lowerâband placement, yielding 2/4 after scaling.

strengths: Application of both descriptive (mean, median, SD) and inferential (MannâWhitney U) statistics
Graphs correctly compare familiar vs. unfamiliar conditions and address the hypothesis
weaknesses: Inferential statistics are inconsistent (zâvalue vs. pâvalue mismatch) and interpretation contradicts the nonâsignificant result
Conclusion incorrectly rejects the null hypothesis despite p > .05, indicating fundamental misinterpretation
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: While the student employs appropriate statistical methods and graphs, significant calculation errors and misinterpretation of pâvalues undermine the analysis. This places the work in the lower performance band, warranting 2/6.

strengths: Strengths and limitations are clearly stated, explained, and directly linked to impacts on validity and reliability
Proposed modifications are explicit, justified, and directly address identified limitations
weaknesses: Discussion of findings remains somewhat surfaceâlevel and lacks deeper theoretical integration or consideration of alternative explanations
Modifications could be strengthened with more specific planning details (e.g., target sample size, power analysis)
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Evaluation of findings is adequately tied to theory (band 3â4) and strengths/limitations and modifications meet the topâband descriptors (bands 5â6). Balancing the underdeveloped discussion depth against strong limitations analysis and justified modifications results in a score of 3/6.

strandSelections.reasoning: Bar charts for both recall and recognition conditions directly compare familiar vs unfamiliar objects and address the hypothesis.  Axes are labelled and values shown; no obvious plotting errors are apparent.  Minor aesthetic issues notwithstanding, this meets the descriptor for a correct graph addressing the hypothesis (5-6 band).  Limited variety of graphs keeps the award to 5.

The student states that familiar objects were recalled more often and links this to schema theory, but claims the hypothesis is supported despite p > .05, demonstrating misinterpretation of significance.  Thus findings are linked to hypothesis but interpretation is partially flawed, matching the 3-4 band; the presence of a substantive error places it at 3.

Both a null and research hypothesis are provided and the IV (recall condition) and DV (memory recall) are identified.  However, neither variable is fully operationalised: the DV is not defined as ânumber of correctly recalled objects,â and the IV is muddied by a second mention of âfamiliar vs unfamiliar objects.â  Because correct variables are stated but not operationalised, the work fits the 3-4 band; minimal operational detail places it at the lower end (3).

Voluntary response sampling is identified, participant pool (multiple grades) is described, and justification (âimprove generalizabilityâ) is offered.  Although brief and without demographics or sample size, a clear explanation of the choice is present, satisfying the 3-4 band at a score of 3.

Schema theory is described with reasonable historical detail (Bartlett, cognitive frameworks guiding perception, encoding and recall).  The link to the investigation is explicitly articulated: the study âwill highlight how we use our schema in the retrieval of episodic memoryâ and contrasts familiar/unfamiliar objects.  Some wording is imprecise, yet the connection is clearly explained, justifying band 5-6; limited depth and a few inaccuracies (e.g., âdetermine, analyse, and interpretâ) cap the score at 5 rather than 6.

The discussion revisits key results (familiar vs unfamiliar recall) and relates them to schema theory, but the analysis remains surface-level and repeats earlier points without deeper theoretical integration or alternative explanations.  This satisfies âdescribed with referenceâ (3-4 band) and receives 3 due to limited depth.

The student clearly identifies the aim â to examine the influence of schema theory on memory recall in a classroom setting â and explicitly justifies its relevance by arguing that a school environment broadens the evidence base beyond Brewer & Treyensâ office study.  The explanation links the investigation to wider theoretical and practical importance (âunderstanding its role in other settingsâ).  While concise, this constitutes a coherent explanation rather than a mere identification, warranting placement in the 5-6 band; minor vagueness about practical applications keeps the score to 5 rather than 6.

Multiple strengths and limitations are identified (design, sampling, procedure) and each is clearly explained and explicitly tied to how it might influence results, demonstrating relevance.  Occasional brevity in justification prevents a 6, but the work clearly meets the 5-6 descriptor; score 5 is awarded.

Materials (familiar classroom objects, unfamiliar tools) and controlled features (fixed viewpoint, time) are mentioned with rationale (âchallenge schemaâ).  The explanation of why objects were chosen moves the work above simple description, fitting the 3-4 band.  Coverage of controlled variables is patchy, keeping the score to 3.

Three specific modifications (use repeated measures, enlarge sample, pre-record object list) are directly linked to previously stated limitations and justified in terms of reducing individual differences, increasing reliability, and minimising experimenter bias.  This fully satisfies the top-band requirement; modest elaboration caps the score at 5 rather than 6.

The student identifies an independent-measures design, explains why it prevents carry-over effects, and notes random assignment, which constitutes an explanation rather than description.  Some methodological phrasing is informal, but the essential reasoning is sound, meriting placement in the 3-4 band; limited depth justifies a score of 3.

Descriptive statistics (mean, median, SD) and inferential testing (Mann-Whitney U with z and p-values) are presented.  Nonetheless, calculation details are fragmented, a z of â0.54885 is inconsistent with the listed p, and interpretation contradicts the non-significant p.  Hence statistics are appropriate but contain errors, aligning with the 3-4 band; breadth of statistics warrants the upper value of 4.

annotations.comment: Key terms such as âframeworksâ and âschemasâ are introduced but lack precise operational definitions; adding clear definitions will enhance conceptual clarity.

The independent variables are described as âquestionnairesâ and âobjectsâ but need clearer specification as ârecall methodâ and âobject familiarityâ to align with standard experimental terminology.
The hypothesis states variables but omits operational details (e.g., define DV as ânumber of correctly recalled objectsâ). Adding these specifics will ensure clear testability.
The justification for replicating in a classroom is effective and highlights practical relevance beyond Brewer & Treyens; this explicit link strengthens the investigation aim.
Voluntary response sampling is described and aims to improve generalizability, but you should acknowledge potential self-selection bias and its impact on validity.

The student provides a reasonable overview of schema theoryâs origins but could refine historical accuracy (e.g., Bartlettâs work dates) and clarify key conceptual nuances to strengthen the theoretical foundation.
The initial introduction could more clearly preview the studyâs aim and its connection to schema theory rather than delaying until page two, improving coherence and guiding the reader early.
The independent-measures design is well chosen to avoid carry-over effects, but terminology is informal; specify âbetween-subjects designâ to align with research conventions.
Materials and controls (vantage point, time limit) are described, but detail on environmental controls (lighting, noise) would strengthen internal validity.
The description of the verbal recognition condition is cut off mid-sentence. Ensure the full procedure is clearly documented to allow replication.
Ethical procedures are well covered with consent and debriefing; this illustrates appropriate consideration of participant welfare.
The procedural summary mentions âvalidityâ but doesnât specify type; clarifying whether ecological or internal validity is meant will improve rigor.
Participant characteristics such as age, gender, and total N are missing; include these demographics to allow evaluation of sample representativeness and power.
The table of abbreviations and descriptive statistics is clear, but the inferential statistics (z and p) appear inconsistent; please recalculate to ensure accuracy.
Graphs effectively illustrate the familiar vs. unfamiliar recall gap, but adding error bars or confidence intervals would convey variability and strengthen interpretation.
The interpretation links the mean differences to schema theory, demonstrating insight into findingsâ implications; this effectively addresses the hypothesis context.
The section highlights low standard deviations to argue consistency; this is an appropriate interpretation of descriptive statistics and strengthens reliability claims.
The verbal recognition graph lacks explicit axis labels for âlikelihood ratingsâ and âobject typesâ; including these would improve clarity and interpretability.
The use of a one-tailed test should be justified given the directional hypothesis; discuss why a one-versus two-tailed test is appropriate.
The conclusion states the null is rejected yet reports p>0.05; this contradiction needs correction to align the inference with the statistical outcome.
Consider reporting an effect size (e.g., Cohenâs d or r) alongside p-values to quantify the magnitude of differences and support practical significance.
The discussion links findings to schema theory effectively but remains at a descriptive level; deepen analysis by considering alternative models or boundary conditions.
The procedure strengths and weaknesses are relevant and clearly linked, but referencing related literature on repeated-measures benefits would strengthen justification.
The proposed modification to increase sample size is well linked to the identified limitation; adding specifics about target N and power analysis would enhance planning rigor.
The conclusion effectively highlights future research directions and educational implications, demonstrating a strong closing argument for the studyâs broader significance.
